serving.natural_language_portion: One medium red apple (approximately 182 g) is a typical single serving, providing roughly 104 kcal and 23 g of carbohydrates with 4.9 g of dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Apples, raw, with skin — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 9003)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for raw apple with skin used as primary nutrient anchor. Core macros cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database (energy 57 kcal, protein 0.2 g, fat 0.0 g, carbohydrate 12.7 g, fibre 2.7 g, sodium 1 mg) which is consistent with USDA values. HPB values retained for energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, and iron as the input source is HPB with confidence 0.95. USDA used to supplement saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamin C, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for apple, raw
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 36 (low) for raw apple is directly listed in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (36 × 23.1 g) / 100 = 8.3. Serving size used: 182 g medium apple providing 23.1 g available carbohydrate (12.7 g/100 g × 1.82). Input record GL of 4.6 appears to be based on a smaller reference portion (approximately 100 g); GL per 182 g serving is 8.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (1 mg/100 g) with meaningful potassium (113 mg/100 g) and soluble fibre supporting blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Red apple is an excellent choice for individuals with hypertension; its near-zero sodium content, potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 113:1, and 2.7 g/100 g dietary fibre collectively support vascular health — consume whole with skin and avoid apple juice, which removes fibre and concentrates sugar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI of 36 and 2.7 g/100 g dietary fibre attenuating postprandial glucose response; intrinsic fructose metabolised more slowly than glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Whole red apple with skin is suitable for most adults with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (1 medium apple per sitting); the low GI (36), soluble fibre matrix, and intact cell structure slow glucose absorption — pairing with a small amount of protein or healthy fat further blunts the glycaemic response, and individual blood glucose monitoring is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.01 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, soluble fibre (pectin) supporting LDL reduction, and polyphenols (quercetin, anthocyanins) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Red apple is cardioprotective: its soluble fibre (pectin) binds bile acids to reduce LDL cholesterol, quercetin and anthocyanins in the red skin provide antioxidant protection against LDL oxidation, and the negligible saturated fat and zero cholesterol content make it consistent with AHA dietary guidelines for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Quercetin and anthocyanins in red skin provide anti-inflammatory polyphenol activity; vitamin C supports collagen synthesis
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red apple may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis through quercetin and anthocyanin content in the skin — consume whole with skin for maximum polyphenol intake, and choose organic where feasible to minimise pesticide exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red apple (Apple, red) is a fresh, whole fruit consumed raw with its bright red skin intact. It has a crisp, crunchy white flesh with a sweet-tart flavour profile that varies by cultivar — common varieties include Fuji, Gala, Red Delicious, and Honeycrisp. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 57 kcal, 12.7 g carbohydrate (of which 12.0 g is intrinsic sugar), 2.7 g dietary fibre, 0.2 g protein, and negligible fat (0.0 g total fat by HPB measurement; trace saturated and unsaturated fatty acids per USDA). The skin is rich in quercetin and anthocyanins — polyphenol antioxidants with documented anti-inflammatory and cardioprotective properties. Its low glycemic index (GI 36) and soluble fibre (pectin) matrix slow glucose absorption, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions. The very low sodium content (1 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium (113 mg/100 g) support blood pressure regulation, making it appropriate for hypertension management. Soluble fibre (pectin) binds bile acids and supports LDL cholesterol reduction, contributing to cardiovascular health. Red apple is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is widely consumed as a snack, in fruit salads, and as a dessert component across global cuisines. Fat_g is confirmed as 0.0 g per HPB Singapore database (trace amounts below detection threshold); saturated, monounsaturated, and polyunsaturated fat values are supplemented from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 9003) and represent trace quantities consistent with a raw fresh fruit.

Is apple suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database with confidence 0.95 (canonical, reviewed record). Values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 9003) for raw apple with skin — both sources are in close agreement. GI value of 36 directly listed in University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). All values are from approved primary sources.
notes: Fat_g is 0.0 g as reported by Singapore HPB Foodo database — this represents a value at or below the detection threshold for a raw fresh fruit; fat is naturally present in trace amounts in apple flesh and skin (confirmed trace per USDA SR Legacy: total fat 0.17 g/100 g). The HPB-reported value of 0.0 g is retained as the primary source value per source hierarchy rules; USDA trace values for saturated fat (0.01 g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.05 g) are included from USDA SR Legacy to satisfy fatty acid completeness requirements. Vitamin B12 (0.0 g), vitamin D (0.0 mcg), selenium (0.0 mcg), and trans fat (0.0 g) are confirmed zero for raw fresh apple per USDA SR Legacy — naturally zero as this is a raw plant food containing no animal-derived nutrients. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — naturally zero as this is a plant food. No -1 sentinel values require documentation as all fields have been populated from approved sources. GI glycemic load per serving recalculated at 8.3 for a 182 g medium apple serving; the input record GL of 4.6 was based on a 100 g reference portion (GL per 100 g = 36 × 12.7 / 100 = 4.6, consistent). Rheumatoid arthritis suitability from input record corrected: the input marked suitable=false with a contradictory reason citing anti-inflammatory polyphenols; the correct assessment is suitable=true (low concern) given quercetin and anthocyanin content — this has been corrected in health_context. Ontology codes: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 A00LC are defensible standard codes for raw apple; SNOMED CT 229958001 is the substance code for apple fruit.
